div.head: The Chargé in the Soviet Union (Kirk) to the Secretary of State
div.p: 182. I am informed that the British Ambassador saw LitvinovMaxim Maximovich Litvinov, People’s Commissar for Foreign Affairs of the Soviet Union. this afternoon at 3:00 o’clock and under instructions from his Government made a proposal along the following lines:

The British Government had taken due note of the statement in Stalin’s recent speech at the Party Congress to the effect that the Soviet Government stood for the support of states, victims of aggressors, who were struggling for their independence (see my telegram No. 94 [99], March 11, 4 p.m.
Foreign Relations, The Soviet Union, 1933–1939, p. 739.). In view of the similarity between this statement and the views recently expressed by the French and
British Governments, the British Government suggested that the Soviet Government on its own initiative issue a public declaration which after referring to the above statement of Stalin and to the French and British views should announce that in conformity with the principle expressed therein, if a country neighbor to the Soviet Union became the victim of aggression and was fighting for its independence the Soviet Government would come to the support of those countries if so desired and in such form as might be suitable.
The Soviet Government has not yet replied to this proposal.
I am further informed that the Soviet Ambassador in LondonIvan Mikhailovich Maisky. is to proceed to Moscow for consultation.
The above information has been furnished by the British Embassy with the request that it be held in strictest confidence.
